reason: The item cleanly tests boundary-aware reasoning about what can and cannot be concluded from an agent discharge, enclosure leakage, and a door-opening-triggered deflagration, separating suppression hold-time effects from cell-level thermal runaway progression. Decoys are largely plausible and each tends to fail by a single overreach (necessity, sufficiency, or exclusive causation), providing good discrimination among experts. Some options invite mild ambiguity around counterfactual certainty (e.g., “could not have occurred” and “necessary precondition”), slightly weakening boundary tightness and discriminative sharpness but not enough to require revision.

The item cleanly tests boundary-aware admissibility: what can and cannot be inferred from equivalence ratio, fill fraction, burning velocity, and a temperature-threshold exceedance. Correct choices avoid over-claiming while decoys tempt common overextensions (structural safety, combustion mode dominance, and causal attribution of heating source). Some decoys (A and E) share a similar “causal overclaim” shape, slightly reducing single-fault diversity and discriminative sharpness.

The item cleanly forces a boundary-aware distinction between (i) enclosure concentration/hold-time failure evidenced by integrity testing and (ii) the inherent inability of gaseous agents to stop cell-level thermal runaway, making the operator’s claim only narrowly defensible. It has strong discriminative power because it penalizes the common conflation of atmospheric flammability suppression with thermal runaway arrest, though the short-answer format slightly reduces control over single-fault decoy behavior and could allow some variability in how respondents frame the two “failure modes.”
